Now I see the pattern. The body is used in format strings like:
```rust
format!("...    pub fn ...() {{\n{}        {}\n    }}\n", ..., prelude, body);
```

So the body is inserted after the prelude with 8-space indentation. If I change  to return multi-line strings, I need to handle the indentation at each line.

Let me also check how the body currently interacts with `wrap_body_with_postamble`:

Searched for text `fn wrap_body_with_postamble` (`**/crates/opencascade-binding-generator/src/codegen/bindings.rs`), 1 result
- [bindings.rs:4805](../crates/opencascade-binding-generator/src/codegen/bindings.rs#L4805)

<details>
<summary>Reading lines 4805-4825 of <a href="../crates/opencascade-binding-generator/src/codegen/bindings.rs#L4805-L4826">bindings.rs</a></summary>

```
pub(super) fn wrap_body_with_postamble(body: &str, postamble: &str, has_return: bool) -> String {
    if postamble.is_empty() {
        return body.to_string();
    }
    if has_return {
        format!("let result_ = {};\n{}        result_", body, postamble)
    } else {
        // Void return: body as statement, then postamble (trim trailing newline for last line)
        let trimmed_postamble = postamble.trim_end_matches('\n');
        format!("{};\n{}", body, trimmed_postamble)
    }
}

/// Build the body expression for a re-export method call.
/// Handles the conversion from FFI raw pointer returns to Rust references/OwnedPtr.
pub(super) fn build_reexport_body(raw_call: &str, rt: Option<&ReturnTypeBinding>) -> String {
    let reexport_type = rt.map(|r| r.rust_reexport_type.as_str());
    let is_enum = rt.and_then(|r| r.enum_rust_type.as_deref());
    let needs_owned_ptr = rt.map_or(false, |r| r.needs_unique_ptr);
    let is_class_ptr_return = rt.map_or(false, |r| r.is_class_ptr_return);
    let is_mut_ref_enum_return = rt.map_or(false, |r| r.is_mut_ref_enum_return);
```
</details>

> 💭 Now I see. The `wrap_body_with_postamble` creates a multi-line body when there's a postamble, but normally the body is a single-line expression. The format string uses `        {}\n` for the body, but `wrap_body_with_postamble` already handles multi-line by putting newlines in the body with `        ` (8-space indent) for postamble lines.
> 
> So the body is used at 8-space indentation. If I return a multi-line body from `build_reexport_body`, the first line would be correctly indented (8 spaces from the format string), but subsequent lines would NOT have the correct indentation.
> 
> 
> I can see now that `wrap_body_with_postamble` already expects multi-line bodies with proper indentation — it formats the postamble with 8-space prefixes. So I need to make sure any additional lines in the body I return also get that same 8-space indentation prefix.
> 
> But looking at how different callers use the body, the indentation context varies: `emit_free_function_wrapper` uses 4 spaces, while class methods use 8 spaces. Rather than embedding indentation in the body itself, I should step back and think about the cleanest approach here.
> 
> The solution is to keep `build_reexport_body` returning a single-line block expression that wraps the FFI call and adds the exception check. For reference returns, I'd use a temporary variable to capture the unsafe result, then call `check_exception()`, then dereference it. For void functions, the block just executes the call and check. For owned pointers and enums, the pattern adapts similarly—the block expression always evaluates to the right type.
> 
> When there's a postamble, the body becomes the right-hand side of a `let result_` assignment, which works perfectly since block expressions can be assigned. This keeps the callers unchanged while cleanly inserting the exception check everywhere it's needed.
> 
> Now examining the C++ side—I need to wrap every extern "C" function body in try/catch that stores exceptions to thread-local state. Reference-returning functions are the tricky part since they currently return `T const&`, which can't represent a null/error state, so I'll need to change those to `T const*` pointers instead.
> 
> Looking at where reference returns show up: CStringParam with `returns_reference`, simple wrappers that call methods, and free functions all use `ffi_cpp_return_type()` to determine the return type. I need to modify that function to convert reference types to pointers at the FFI boundary, then update the return statement formatting to handle the pointer conversion properly.
> 
> Modifying return type handling... I'm refactoring the return type handling to use the standard `ffi_cpp_return_type()` and `format_cpp_return_stmt()` functions instead of special-casing the `returns_reference` branch, which should consolidate the logic for converting references to pointers. Now I need to figure out what default return value to use after the catch block.
> 
> I'm defining a helper function that computes the appropriate zero-initialized default based on the C++ return type — pointers get `nullptr`, numeric types get `0`, booleans get `false`, and everything else gets brace initialization `{}` for safe zero-initialization. Since `ffi_cpp_return_type()` already converts references to pointers, those will naturally match the pointer case.
> 
> Now I'm mapping out the full implementation plan systematically, starting with adding the exception handling module and then updating the binding generator to apply these changes throughout.
> 
> I need to modify `ffi_cpp_return_type()` to convert reference returns to pointers, then update `format_cpp_return_stmt()` to wrap reference returns with address-of operators. I'll add helper functions for try/catch emission and modify `emit_cpp_class()` to wrap all function bodies in exception handling at each emission site.
> 
> For the multi-line functions, I'll expand the try/catch around the existing body, and for one-liners I'll need to convert them to multi-line format. I should also include `<typeinfo>` and `<cstring>` in the generated wrappers, and handle the special case where `CStringParam` currently formats reference returns manually — I need to unify this with `ffi_cpp_return_type()`.
> 
> For complex cases like `MutRefEnumParam`, the entire preamble, method call, and postamble go inside the try block so that if the method throws, the catch handler stores the exception and we return a default value. For void functions with postamble, if an exception occurs the postamble is skipped, which is fine since the Rust side will panic anyway.
> 
> I'm deciding which functions to skip wrapping: destructors (can't recover from throwing destructors), sizeof helpers (constexpr, can't throw), and upcasts (can't throw). Everything else gets wrapped.
> 
> Now I'm realizing the `CStringParam` section has special handling for reference returns that bypasses `ffi_cpp_return_type()`, so I need to unify this — the `returns_reference` branch should use `ffi_cpp_return_type()` for the signature and `format_cpp_return_stmt()` for the body, making it identical to the general case and allowing me to remove the special case.
> 
> For the actual changes, I'll keep the refactoring minimal and focused on adding exception handling. I need to wrap the method calls in try/catch blocks at the constructor, ByValueReturn, and CStringParam emission sites, while also fixing the reference return handling to use the new conversion logic. Still writing error handling... Writing the helper function...
> 
> Now I'm ready to start implementing the full solution with this design in place.

{

      "filePath": "crates/opencascade-binding-generator/src/codegen/bindings.rs",
      "lineHashes": "360:do,361:dt,362:sj,363:bb,364:ui,365:ol,366:ea,367:ag,368:lm,369:cy,370:ax",
      "content": "    pub fn ffi_cpp_return_type(&self) -> String {\n        if self.needs_unique_ptr {\n            format!(\"{}*\", self.cpp_type)\n        } else if self.is_mut_ref_enum_return {\n            \"int32_t*\".to_string()\n        } else if self.enum_cpp_name.is_some() {\n            \"int32_t\".to_string()\n        } else if self.cpp_type.contains('&') {\n            // Convert reference returns to pointer returns for exception safety.\n            // At the ABI level, C++ references and pointers are identical.\n            self.cpp_type.replace('&', \"*\")\n        } else {\n            self.cpp_type.clone()\n        }\n    }"
    },
    {
      "filePath": "crates/opencascade-binding-generator/src/codegen/bindings.rs",
      "lineHashes": "377:is,378:el,379:ta,380:xf,381:bc,382:ol,383:ww,384:ag,385:jt,386:dv,387:dd,388:dy,389:ag,390:ro,391:hk,392:ap,393:sr,394:ud,395:sh,396:hl,397:rs,398:ag,399:em,400:cy,401:ax",
      "content": "    pub fn format_cpp_return_stmt(&self, call_expr: &str, assign_to_variable: bool) -> String {\n        let is_ref_return = !self.needs_unique_ptr\n            && !self.is_mut_ref_enum_return\n            && self.enum_cpp_name.is_none()\n            && self.cpp_type.contains('&');\n\n        let expr = if self.is_mut_ref_enum_return {\n            format!(\"reinterpret_cast<int32_t*>(&({}))\", call_expr)\n        } else if self.needs_unique_ptr {\n            format!(\"new {}({})\", self.cpp_type, call_expr)\n        } else if self.enum_cpp_name.is_some() {\n            format!(\"static_cast<int32_t>({})\", call_expr)\n        } else if is_ref_return {\n            // Reference return converted to pointer: take address of result\n            format!(\"&({})\", call_expr)\n        } else {\n            call_expr.to_string()\n        };\n\n        if assign_to_variable {\n            format!(\"auto result_ = {};\", expr)\n        } else {\n            format!(\"return {};\", expr)\n        }\n    }"
    }
  ]
}

      "content": "    writeln!(output).unwrap();\n\n    // Exception handling infrastructure.\n    // Thread-local storage for C++ exception info, checked by Rust after each FFI call.\n    output.push_str(r#\"\n// ========================\n// Exception handling\n// ========================\n\nnamespace {\nstruct OcctExceptionInfo {\n    bool pending = false;\n    char message[2048] = {};\n    char type_name[256] = {};\n};\nthread_local OcctExceptionInfo occt_last_exception;\n}\n\nstatic void occt_store_exception(const char* msg, const char* type_name) {\n    auto& ex = occt_last_exception;\n    ex.pending = true;\n    if (msg) {\n        std::strncpy(ex.message, msg, sizeof(ex.message) - 1);\n        ex.message[sizeof(ex.message) - 1] = '\\0';\n    } else {\n        ex.message[0] = '\\0';\n    }\n    if (type_name) {\n        std::strncpy(ex.type_name, type_name, sizeof(ex.type_name) - 1);\n        ex.type_name[sizeof(ex.type_name) - 1] = '\\0';\n    } else {\n        ex.type_name[0] = '\\0';\n    }\n}\n\nextern \"C\" bool occt_has_pending_exception() {\n    return occt_last_exception.pending;\n}\n\nextern \"C\" const char* occt_pending_exception_message() {\n    return occt_last_exception.message;\n}\n\nextern \"C\" const char* occt_pending_exception_type() {\n    return occt_last_exception.type_name;\n}\n\nextern \"C\" void occt_clear_exception() {\n    occt_last_exception.pending = false;\n}\n\n#define OCCT_CATCH_AND_STORE \\\n    catch (const Standard_Failure& e) { \\\n        occt_store_exception(e.GetMessageString(), typeid(e).name()); \\\n    } catch (const std::exception& e) { \\\n        occt_store_exception(e.what(), typeid(e).name()); \\\n    } catch (...) { \\\n        occt_store_exception(\"unknown C++ exception\", \"unknown\"); \\\n    }\n\n\"#);",

/// Emit C++ wrapper code for a single class from pre-computed ClassBindings.
///
/// Produces C++ wrapper code for a class
/// and its 10+ sub-functions, but consumes the pre-computed IR instead
/// of re-deriving decisions.
pub fn emit_cpp_class(bindings: &ClassBindings) -> String {
    use std::fmt::Write;

    let mut output = String::new();
    let ffi_cn = &bindings.cpp_name;  // Rust-safe flattened name (for FFI function names)
    let cn = &bindings.cpp_qualified_name;  // C++ qualified name (for C++ type expressions)
```

Now I need to modify each emission site to add try/catch. This is a lot of individual edits. Let me write a comprehensive script to do this. The approach for each pattern:

**For single-statement bodies (most common case):**
- After  → add  → add `    try {`  
- Keep body line as-is
- Replace `}` with `    } OCCT_CATCH_AND_STORE` + optional default return + `}`

**For multi-statement bodies (MutRefEnumParam, etc.):**
- After  → add `    try {`
- Keep all body lines
- Before final `}` → add `    } OCCT_CATCH_AND_STORE` + optional default return

            "extern \"C\" {ret_cpp}* {fn_name}({all_params}) {{",
            fn_name = wm.ffi_fn_name
        )
        .unwrap();
        writeln!(
            output,
            "    return new {ret_cpp}(self_->{method}({args_str}));",
            method = wm.cpp_method_name
        )
        .unwrap();
        writeln!(output, "}}").unwrap();
    }

    //   2. return_by_value
    //   3. c_string_param

    // 3. CStringParam wrapper methods
    for wm in bindings
        .wrapper_methods
        .iter()
        .filter(|m| m.wrapper_kind == WrapperKind::CStringParam)
    {
        let self_param = if wm.is_const {
            format!("const {cn}* self_")
        } else {
            format!("{cn}* self_")
        };

        let other_params = wm
            .params
            .iter()
            .map(|p| format!("{} {}", p.cpp_type, p.cpp_name))
            .collect::<Vec<_>>()
            .join(", ");
        let params = if other_params.is_empty() {
            self_param
        } else {
            format!("{}, {}", self_param, other_params)
        };
        let args_str = wm
            .params
            .iter()
            .map(|p| p.cpp_arg_expr.as_str())
            .collect::<Vec<_>>()
            .join(", ");

        // Determine return behaviour
        let returns_cstring = wm
            .return_type
            .as_ref()
            .map(|rt| rt.cpp_type == "const char*")
            .unwrap_or(false);
        let returns_reference = wm
            .return_type
            .as_ref()
            .map(|rt| rt.cpp_type.contains('&'))
            .unwrap_or(false);

        if returns_cstring {
            writeln!(
                output,
                "extern \"C\" const char* {fn_name}({params}) {{",
                fn_name = wm.ffi_fn_name
            )
            .unwrap();
            writeln!(
                output,
                "    return self_->{method}({args_str});",
                method = wm.cpp_method_name
            )
            .unwrap();
        } else if returns_reference {
            let ret_cpp = &wm.return_type.as_ref().unwrap().cpp_type;
            writeln!(
                output,
                "extern \"C\" {ret_cpp} {fn_name}({params}) {{",
                fn_name = wm.ffi_fn_name
            )
            .unwrap();
            writeln!(
                output,
                "    return self_->{method}({args_str});",
                method = wm.cpp_method_name
            )
            .unwrap();
        } else if wm.return_type.is_none() {
            writeln!(
                output,
                "extern \"C\" void {fn_name}({params}) {{",
                fn_name = wm.ffi_fn_name
            )
            .unwrap();
            writeln!(
                output,
                "    self_->{method}({args_str});",
                method = wm.cpp_method_name
            )
            .unwrap();
        } else {
            let rt = wm.return_type.as_ref().unwrap();
            let ret_cpp = &rt.cpp_type;
            writeln!(
                output,
                "extern \"C\" {ret_cpp} {fn_name}({params}) {{",
                fn_name = wm.ffi_fn_name
            )
            .unwrap();
            let call_expr = format!("self_->{}({})", wm.cpp_method_name, args_str);
            writeln!(output, "    {}", rt.format_cpp_return_stmt(&call_expr, false)).unwrap();
        }
        writeln!(output, "}}").unwrap();
    }

    for wm in bindings

<summary>Reading lines 4206-4300 of <a href="../crates/opencascade-binding-generator/src/codegen/bindings.rs#L4206-L4301">bindings.rs</a></summary>

```
    // 4e. MutRefEnumParam wrapper methods
    // These have &mut enum output parameters. The wrapper:
    // 1. Takes int32_t& at the FFI boundary
    // 2. Creates local enum variables from the int32_t values
    // 3. Calls the original method
    // 4. Writes back the enum values as int32_t
    for wm in bindings
        .wrapper_methods
        .iter()
        .filter(|m| m.wrapper_kind == WrapperKind::MutRefEnumParam)
    {
        let self_param = if wm.is_const {
            format!("const {cn}* self_")
        } else {
            format!("{cn}* self_")
        };

        let other_params = wm
            .params
            .iter()
            .map(|p| format!("{} {}", p.cpp_type, p.cpp_name))
            .collect::<Vec<_>>()
            .join(", ");
        let params = if other_params.is_empty() {
            self_param
        } else {
            format!("{}, {}", self_param, other_params)
        };

        // Determine return type
        let ret_type_cpp = wm.return_type.as_ref()
            .map(|rt| rt.ffi_cpp_return_type())
            .unwrap_or_else(|| "void".to_string());

        writeln!(
            output,
            "extern \"C\" {} {fn_name}({params}) {{",
            ret_type_cpp,
            fn_name = wm.ffi_fn_name
        )
        .unwrap();

        // Emit preamble: create local enum variables from int32_t input values
        for p in &wm.params {
            if let Some(ref enum_name) = p.mut_ref_enum_cpp_name {
                writeln!(
                    output,
                    "    auto {local} = static_cast<{enum_name}>({param});",
                    local = p.cpp_arg_expr,
                    param = p.cpp_name,
                )
                .unwrap();
            }
        }

        // Emit the call
        let args_str = wm
            .params
            .iter()
            .map(|p| p.cpp_arg_expr.as_str())
            .collect::<Vec<_>>()
            .join(", ");

        if let Some(ref rt) = wm.return_type {
            let call_expr = format!("self_->{}({})", wm.cpp_method_name, args_str);
            writeln!(output, "    {}", rt.format_cpp_return_stmt(&call_expr, true)).unwrap();
        } else {
            writeln!(
                output,
                "    self_->{method}({args_str});",
                method = wm.cpp_method_name,
            )
            .unwrap();
        }

        // Emit postamble: write back enum values to int32_t& output params
        for p in &wm.params {
            if let Some(ref _enum_name) = p.mut_ref_enum_cpp_name {
                writeln!(
                    output,
                    "    {param} = static_cast<int32_t>({local});",
                    param = p.cpp_name,
                    local = p.cpp_arg_expr,
                )
                .unwrap();
            }
        }

        // Emit return
        if wm.return_type.is_some() {
            writeln!(output, "    return result_;").unwrap();
        }

        writeln!(output, "}}").unwrap();
    }
```
</details>

        for func in namespace_functions {
            let wrapper_name = &func.cpp_wrapper_name;

            let params_cpp: Vec<String> = func.params.iter()
                .map(|p| format!("{} {}", p.cpp_type, p.cpp_name))

                ).unwrap();
            }
        }
        writeln!(output).unwrap();
    }
}

```

/// Compute the default C++ return value for error paths in exception handling.
/// Returns `None` for void functions.
fn cpp_error_return_value(cpp_return_type: &str) -> Option<&'static str> {
    if cpp_return_type == "void" {
        None
    } else if cpp_return_type.contains('*') {
        Some("nullptr")
    } else if cpp_return_type == "bool" {
        Some("false")
    } else {
        // Covers int32_t, double, float, size_t, Standard_Real, etc.
        Some("0")
    }
}

/// Wrap a reexport body expression with &mut enum writeback postamble.
/// When postamble is non-empty, splits into multi-statement body:
///   let result_ = <body>;
///   <postamble>
///   result_
/// For void returns (has_return=false):
///   <body>;
///   <postamble trimmed>
pub(super) fn wrap_body_with_postamble(body: &str, postamble: &str, has_return: bool) -> String {
    if postamble.is_empty() {
        return body.to_string();
    }
    if has_return {
        format!("let result_ = {};\n{}        result_", body, postamble)
    } else {
        // Void return: body as statement, then postamble (trim trailing newline for last line)
        let trimmed_postamble = postamble.trim_end_matches('\n');
        format!("{};\n{}", body, trimmed_postamble)
    }
}

/// Build the body expression for a re-export method call.
/// Handles the conversion from FFI raw pointer returns to Rust references/OwnedPtr.
pub(super) fn build_reexport_body(raw_call: &str, rt: Option<&ReturnTypeBinding>) -> String {
    let reexport_type = rt.map(|r| r.rust_reexport_type.as_str());
    let is_enum = rt.and_then(|r| r.enum_rust_type.as_deref());
    let needs_owned_ptr = rt.map_or(false, |r| r.needs_unique_ptr);
    let is_class_ptr_return = rt.map_or(false, |r| r.is_class_ptr_return);
    let is_mut_ref_enum_return = rt.map_or(false, |r| r.is_mut_ref_enum_return);
    if is_mut_ref_enum_return {
        // &mut enum return: FFI returns *mut i32, cast to *mut EnumType and dereference
        if let Some(enum_type) = is_enum {
            return format!("unsafe {{ &mut *({} as *mut {}) }}", raw_call, enum_type);
        } else {
            return format!("unsafe {{ &mut *({}) }}", raw_call);
        }
    }
    if is_class_ptr_return {
        // Class pointer returns are bound as Option<&T> / Option<&mut T>.
        // The FFI returns a raw pointer; we null-check and convert.
```
</details>

<details>
<summary>Reading lines 4875-4910 of <a href="../crates/opencascade-binding-generator/src/codegen/bindings.rs#L4875-L4911">bindings.rs</a></summary>

```
        // The FFI returns a raw pointer; we null-check and convert.
        if let Some(rt) = reexport_type {
            if rt.starts_with("Option<&mut ") {
                return format!("{{ let ptr = unsafe {{ {} }}; if ptr.is_null() {{ None }} else {{ Some(unsafe {{ &mut *ptr }}) }} }}", raw_call);
            }
        }
        return format!("{{ let ptr = unsafe {{ {} }}; if ptr.is_null() {{ None }} else {{ Some(unsafe {{ &*ptr }}) }} }}", raw_call);
    }
    if let Some(enum_type) = is_enum {
        format!("unsafe {{ {}::try_from({}).unwrap() }}", enum_type, raw_call)
    } else if needs_owned_ptr {
        format!("unsafe {{ crate::OwnedPtr::from_raw({}) }}", raw_call)
    } else if let Some(rt) = reexport_type {
        if rt == "std::string::String" {
            format!("unsafe {{ std::ffi::CStr::from_ptr({}).to_string_lossy().into_owned() }}", raw_call)
        } else if rt.starts_with("&mut ") {
            format!("unsafe {{ &mut *({}) }}", raw_call)
        } else if rt.starts_with('&') {
            format!("unsafe {{ &*({}) }}", raw_call)
        } else {
            format!("unsafe {{ {} }}", raw_call)
        }
    } else {
        format!("unsafe {{ {} }}", raw_call)
    }
}

```
//! Tests for C++ exception handling across the FFI boundary.
//!
//! These tests verify that OCCT C++ exceptions are caught by the generated
//! wrappers and converted into Rust panics with meaningful messages.

/// Calling `TopoDS::Face()` on a Wire shape throws `Standard_TypeMismatch`
/// in C++. Verify that this is caught and turned into a Rust panic.
#[test]
#[should_panic(expected = "Standard_TypeMismatch")]
fn face_shape_on_wire_panics_with_type_mismatch() {
    // Create a wire (not a face)
    let edge = opencascade_sys::b_rep_builder_api::MakeEdge::new_gp_pnt_gp_pnt(
        &opencascade_sys::gp::Pnt::new(0.0, 0.0, 0.0),
        &opencascade_sys::gp::Pnt::new(1.0, 0.0, 0.0),
    );
    let mut wire_builder = opencascade_sys::b_rep_builder_api::MakeWire::new();
    wire_builder.add_edge(edge.edge());
    let wire_shape = wire_builder.shape();

    // This should panic because wire_shape is a Wire, not a Face
    let _face = opencascade_sys::topo_ds::face_shape(wire_shape);
}

```
</details>

```
        }
    }

    headers.sort();
    headers.dedup();

    for header in &headers {
        writeln!(output, "#include <{}>", header).unwrap();
    }
    writeln!(output).unwrap();

    // Exception handling infrastructure.
    // Thread-local storage for C++ exception info, checked by Rust after each FFI call.
    output.push_str(r#"
// ========================
// Exception handling
// ========================

namespace {
struct OcctExceptionInfo {
    bool pending = false;
    char message[2048] = {};
    char type_name[256] = {};
};
thread_local OcctExceptionInfo occt_last_exception;
}

static void occt_store_exception(const char* msg, const char* type_name) {
    auto& ex = occt_last_exception;
    ex.pending = true;
    if (msg) {
        std::strncpy(ex.message, msg, sizeof(ex.message) - 1);
        ex.message[sizeof(ex.message) - 1] = '\0';
    } else {
        ex.message[0] = '\0';
    }
    if (type_name) {
        std::strncpy(ex.type_name, type_name, sizeof(ex.type_name) - 1);
        ex.type_name[sizeof(ex.type_name) - 1] = '\0';
    } else {
        ex.type_name[0] = '\0';
    }
}

extern "C" bool occt_has_pending_exception() {
    return occt_last_exception.pending;
}

extern "C" const char* occt_pending_exception_message() {
    return occt_last_exception.message;
}

extern "C" const char* occt_pending_exception_type() {
    return occt_last_exception.type_name;
}

extern "C" void occt_clear_exception() {
    occt_last_exception.pending = false;
}

#define OCCT_CATCH_AND_STORE \
    catch (const Standard_Failure& e) { \
        occt_store_exception(e.GetMessageString(), typeid(e).name()); \
    } catch (const std::exception& e) { \
        occt_store_exception(e.what(), typeid(e).name()); \
    } catch (...) { \
        occt_store_exception("unknown C++ exception", "unknown"); \
    }

"#);

    // Generate typedefs for template instantiation aliases.
    // These MUST come before Handle typedefs since handles reference the alias names.

{
  "edits": [
    {
      "content": "// ========================\n// Exception handling\n// ========================\n\n#include <cxxabi.h>\n\nnamespace {\nstruct OcctExceptionInfo {\n    bool pending = false;\n    char message[2048] = {};\n    char type_name[256] = {};\n};\nthread_local OcctExceptionInfo occt_last_exception;\n}\n\nstatic void occt_store_exception(const char* msg, const char* type_name) {\n    auto& ex = occt_last_exception;\n    ex.pending = true;\n    if (msg) {\n        std::strncpy(ex.message, msg, sizeof(ex.message) - 1);\n        ex.message[sizeof(ex.message) - 1] = '\\0';\n    } else {\n        ex.message[0] = '\\0';\n    }\n    // Demangle C++ type name for readable panic messages\n    if (type_name) {\n        int status = 0;\n        char* demangled = abi::__cxa_demangle(type_name, nullptr, nullptr, &status);\n        if (status == 0 && demangled) {\n            std::strncpy(ex.type_name, demangled, sizeof(ex.type_name) - 1);\n            ex.type_name[sizeof(ex.type_name) - 1] = '\\0';\n            std::free(demangled);\n        } else {\n            std::strncpy(ex.type_name, type_name, sizeof(ex.type_name) - 1);\n            ex.type_name[sizeof(ex.type_name) - 1] = '\\0';\n            std::free(demangled);\n        }\n    } else {\n        ex.type_name[0] = '\\0';\n    }\n}",

> 💭 Now I understand CXX's approach. Key findings:
> 
> 1. **CXX's approach**: When a function `throws`, CXX wraps the call in `::rust::behavior::trycatch()` which catches exceptions and returns a `::rust::repr::PtrLen` struct. The `ok` field is null when no error; when there's an error, `err` contains a pointer+length to the error message. On the Rust side, the shim returns this `PtrLen` and Rust checks `if error$.ptr` is non-null.
> 
> 2. **Key insight**: CXX doesn't use thread-local storage! It returns the error info directly in the function's return value. For void functions, it returns a `PtrLen`. For non-void functions, it uses an indirect return (out parameter) for the actual return value, and the function return value becomes the `PtrLen` error.
>    
> For improving our implementation, I can use `#[track_caller]` on `check_exception()` to make panic locations point to the actual caller rather than the helper function itself—this is the idiomatic Rust approach. I should also remove `#[inline]` and add `#[cold]` to keep the error path out of the hot code path.
> 
> Actually, splitting into two functions makes more sense: `check_exception()` stays lightweight and inlined to just check the boolean, while a separate `throw_pending_exception()` function handles the expensive work of reading the exception info and panicking with `#[cold]` and `#[inline(never)]`. For the no-exception case, this keeps the overhead minimal since we're only doing a cheap boolean check on the hot path.
> 
> As for avoiding the extra function call entirely, I could have C++ wrappers return a status code instead, but that would require restructuring return values and using out-parameters for actual results—probably too invasive for the benefit.
> 
> A middle ground: have C++ functions return an exception status alongside their normal result. For pointer returns, `nullptr` already signals an exception since we convert references to pointers. For void functions, return a bool. For scalar returns, use a struct or out-parameter. This avoids thread-local access in the common case without completely rewriting signatures.
> 
> Actually, the real issue is that `check_exception()` requires an FFI call to read a thread-local every time. Since exceptions are rare, I could optimize by checking the return value first—if a function that originally returned a reference gives us `nullptr`, that's definitely an exception, so we only call `check_exception()` when needed. For functions that legitimately return pointers, `nullptr` is ambiguous, but we could still use this heuristic for the reference-to-pointer conversions.

> The binding generator already tracks whether returns were references or pointers, so the Rust side knows which functions can safely use this optimization. > Looking at what CXX actually does—it returns a struct containing both the error status and the actual value, with the C++ side catching exceptions and storing error messages. ```
//! C++ exception handling for the OCCT FFI boundary.
//!
//! When a C++ OCCT function throws an exception (typically a `Standard_Failure`
//! subclass), the generated wrapper catches it and stores the exception info
//! in a thread-local. After every FFI call, the generated Rust wrappers call
//! `check_exception()` which panics if an exception was caught.

use std::ffi::{c_char, CStr};

extern "C" {
    fn occt_has_pending_exception() -> bool;
    fn occt_pending_exception_message() -> *const c_char;
    fn occt_pending_exception_type() -> *const c_char;
    fn occt_clear_exception();
}

/// Check for pending OCCT C++ exceptions and panic if one is found.
///
/// Called after every FFI call to convert C++ exceptions to Rust panics.
/// The panic message includes the C++ exception type and message.
/// Set `RUST_BACKTRACE=1` to see the full stack trace.
#[inline]
pub fn check_exception() {
    if unsafe { occt_has_pending_exception() } {
        let msg = unsafe {
            let ptr = occt_pending_exception_message();
            if ptr.is_null() {
                "<null>"
            } else {
                CStr::from_ptr(ptr).to_str().unwrap_or("<invalid UTF-8>")
            }
        };
        let ty = unsafe {
            let ptr = occt_pending_exception_type();
            if ptr.is_null() {
                "<null>"
            } else {
                CStr::from_ptr(ptr).to_str().unwrap_or("<invalid UTF-8>")
            }
        };
        unsafe { occt_clear_exception() };
        panic!("OCCT C++ exception: {ty}: {msg}");
    }
}

```
</details>

```
//! Tests for C++ exception handling across the FFI boundary.
//!
//! These tests verify that OCCT C++ exceptions are caught by the generated
//! wrappers and converted into Rust panics with meaningful messages.

/// Calling `TopoDS::Face()` on a non-Face shape throws `Standard_TypeMismatch`
/// in C++. Verify that this is caught and turned into a Rust panic.
#[test]
#[should_panic(expected = "Standard_TypeMismatch")]
fn face_shape_on_compound_panics_with_type_mismatch() {
    // Create a box — its .shape() returns a Compound (not a Face)
    let mut make_box = opencascade_sys::b_rep_prim_api::MakeBox::new_real3(10.0, 20.0, 30.0);
    let compound_shape = make_box.shape();

    // This should panic because compound_shape is a Compound, not a Face
    let _face = opencascade_sys::topo_ds::face_shape(compound_shape);
}
```
</details>

<summary>Reading lines 4859-4940 of <a href="../crates/opencascade-binding-generator/src/codegen/bindings.rs#L4859-L4941">bindings.rs</a></summary>

```
pub(super) fn build_reexport_body(raw_call: &str, rt: Option<&ReturnTypeBinding>) -> String {
    let reexport_type = rt.map(|r| r.rust_reexport_type.as_str());
    let is_enum = rt.and_then(|r| r.enum_rust_type.as_deref());
    let needs_owned_ptr = rt.map_or(false, |r| r.needs_unique_ptr);
    let is_class_ptr_return = rt.map_or(false, |r| r.is_class_ptr_return);
    let is_mut_ref_enum_return = rt.map_or(false, |r| r.is_mut_ref_enum_return);

    // Void return: call, check, done
    if rt.is_none() || reexport_type.is_none() {
        return format!("{{ unsafe {{ {} }}; crate::check_exception(); }}", raw_call);
    }

    // All non-void paths: call the FFI, check for exception, then convert result.
    // The check_exception() call panics before we use the (potentially garbage) result
    // if a C++ exception was caught.

    if is_mut_ref_enum_return {
        // &mut enum return: FFI returns *mut i32, cast to *mut EnumType and dereference
        if let Some(enum_type) = is_enum {
            return format!("{{ let __result = unsafe {{ {} }}; crate::check_exception(); unsafe {{ &mut *(__result as *mut {}) }} }}", raw_call, enum_type);
        } else {
            return format!("{{ let __result = unsafe {{ {} }}; crate::check_exception(); unsafe {{ &mut *(__result) }} }}", raw_call);
        }
    }
    if is_class_ptr_return {
        // Class pointer returns are bound as Option<&T> / Option<&mut T>.
        // The FFI returns a raw pointer; we null-check and convert.
        if let Some(rty) = reexport_type {
            if rty.starts_with("Option<&mut ") {
                return format!("{{ let __result = unsafe {{ {} }}; crate::check_exception(); if __result.is_null() {{ None }} else {{ Some(unsafe {{ &mut *__result }}) }} }}", raw_call);
            }
        }
        return format!("{{ let __result = unsafe {{ {} }}; crate::check_exception(); if __result.is_null() {{ None }} else {{ Some(unsafe {{ &*__result }}) }} }}", raw_call);
    }
    if let Some(enum_type) = is_enum {
        return format!("{{ let __result = unsafe {{ {} }}; crate::check_exception(); {}::try_from(__result).unwrap() }}", raw_call, enum_type);
    }
    if needs_owned_ptr {
        return format!("{{ let __result = unsafe {{ {} }}; crate::check_exception(); unsafe {{ crate::OwnedPtr::from_raw(__result) }} }}", raw_call);
    }
    if let Some(rty) = reexport_type {
        if rty == "std::string::String" {
            return format!("{{ let __result = unsafe {{ {} }}; crate::check_exception(); unsafe {{ std::ffi::CStr::from_ptr(__result) }}.to_string_lossy().into_owned() }}", raw_call);
        } else if rty.starts_with("&mut ") {
            return format!("{{ let __result = unsafe {{ {} }}; crate::check_exception(); unsafe {{ &mut *(__result) }} }}", raw_call);
        } else if rty.starts_with('&') {
            return format!("{{ let __result = unsafe {{ {} }}; crate::check_exception(); unsafe {{ &*(__result) }} }}", raw_call);
        }
    }
    // Simple passthrough (primitives, bool, etc.)
    format!("{{ let __result = unsafe {{ {} }}; crate::check_exception(); __result }}", raw_call)
}

pub fn emit_reexport_class(bindings: &ClassBindings, module_name: &str) -> String {
    let cn = &bindings.cpp_name;
    let short_name = &bindings.short_name;

    let mut output = String::new();

    // Source attribution + doc comment for the class
    let class_source = format_source_attribution(
        &bindings.source_header,
        bindings.source_line,
        cn,
    );
    output.push_str(&format!("/// {}\n", class_source));
    if let Some(ref comment) = bindings.doc_comment {
        for line in comment.lines() {
            let trimmed = line.trim();
            if trimmed.is_empty() {
                output.push_str("///\n");
            } else {
                output.push_str(&format!("/// {}\n", trimmed));
            }
        }
    }

    // Type alias re-export
    output.push_str(&format!(
        "pub use crate::ffi::{} as

        } else {
            // Regular constructor: delegates to ffi function
            let prelude = cstr_prelude_params(&ctor.params, "        ");
            let unsafe_kw = if ctor.is_unsafe { "unsafe " } else { "" };
            impl_methods.push(format!(
                "{}    pub {}fn {}({}) -> crate::OwnedPtr<Self> {{\n{}        {{\n            let __result = unsafe {{ crate::ffi::{}({}) }};\n            crate::check_exception();\n            unsafe {{ crate::OwnedPtr::from_raw(__result) }}\n        }}\n    }}\n",
                doc,
                unsafe_kw,
                ctor.impl_method_name,
                params.join(", "),

    // 4. Upcast methods
    for up in &bindings.upcasts {
        let ret_type = if up.base_module == module_name {
            up.base_short_name.clone()
        } else {
            let rust_mod = module_graph::module_to_rust_name(&up.base_module);
            format!("crate::{}::{}", rust_mod, up.base_short_name)
        };

        impl_methods.push(format!(
            "    /// Upcast to {}\n    pub fn {}(&self) -> &{} {{\n        {{\n            let __result = unsafe {{ crate::ffi::{}(self as *const Self) }};\n            crate::check_exception();\n            unsafe {{ &*__result }}\n        }}\n    }}\n",
            up.base_class, up.impl_method_name, ret_type, up.ffi_fn_name
        ));

        impl_methods.push(format!(
            "    /// Upcast to {} (mutable)\n    pub fn {}_mut(&mut self) -> &mut {} {{\n        {{\n            let __result = unsafe {{ crate::ffi::{}(self as *mut Self) }};\n            crate::check_exception();\n            unsafe {{ &mut *__result }}\n        }}\n    }}\n",
            up.base_class, up.impl_method_name, ret_type, up.ffi_fn_name_mut
        ));
    }

    // 5. to_owned
    if bindings.has_to_owned {
        let ffi_fn_name = format!("{}_to_owned", cn);
        impl_methods.push(format!(
            "    /// Clone into a new OwnedPtr via copy constructor\n    pub fn to_owned(&self) -> crate::OwnedPtr<Self> {{\n        {{\n            let __result = unsafe {{ crate::ffi::{}(self as *const Self) }};\n            crate::check_exception();\n            unsafe {{ crate::OwnedPtr::from_raw(__result) }}\n        }}\n    }}\n",
            ffi_fn_name
        ));
    }

    // 6. to_handle
    if bindings.has_to_handle {
        let ffi_fn_name = format!("{}_to_handle", cn);
        let handle_type_name = type_mapping::handle_type_name(cn);
        impl_methods.push(format!(
            "    /// Wrap in a Handle (reference-counted smart pointer)\n    pub fn to_handle(obj: crate::OwnedPtr<Self>) -> crate::OwnedPtr<crate::ffi::{}> {{\n        {{\n            let __result = unsafe {{ crate::ffi::{}(obj.into_raw()) }};\n            crate::check_exception();\n            unsafe {{ crate::OwnedPtr::from_raw(__result) }}\n        }}\n    }}\n",
            handle_type_name, ffi_fn_name
        ));
    }

    // 7. Inherited methods (delegates to inherited wrapper free functions)
    for im in &bindings.inherited_methods {
        let self_param = if im.is_const {
            "&self".to_string()
        } else {
            "&mut self".to_string()
        };

        let self_arg = if im.is_const {
            "self as *const Self".to_string()
        } else {
            "self as *mut Self".to_string()
        };

        let params: Vec<String> = std::iter::once(self_param)
            .chain(
                im.params
                    .iter()
                    .map(|p| format!("{}: {}", safe_param_name(&p.rust_name), p.rust_reexport_type)),
            )
            .collect();
        let param_names: Vec<String> = im.params.iter().map(|p| safe_param_name(&p.rust_name)).collect();

Now I have a clear picture. Let me design the implementation plan:

**Key design decisions:**

1. **Panic locations**: Use `#[track_caller]` on both `check_exception()` and `throw_pending_exception()` — the `#[track_caller]` propagation chain ensures `panic!()` shows the wrapper's file/line
2. **Code size**: Split into `check_exception()` (`#[inline(always)]`) for the hot path (just a bool check) and `throw_pending_exception()` (`#[cold] #[inline(never)]`) for the cold panic path
3. **Efficiency**: For pointer-returning wrappers (ref returns, constructors, OwnedPtr), use a local null check instead of calling `check_exception()`. Only call `check_exception()` for void/primitive/enum returns.
4. **Function names**: Pass the FFI function name as `&str` to identify the function in panic messages

{
  "edits": [
    {
      "filePath": "crates/opencascade-sys/src/exception.rs",
      "lineHashes": "1:bw,2:at,3:ml,4:nu,5:ip,6:ee,7:dd,8:fq,9:dd,10:bo,11:gr,12:uc,13:vj,14:sc,15:io,16:dd,17:vb,18:kn,19:tc,20:lc,21:cr,22:nj,23:ud,24:sh,25:xg,26:at,27:gw,28:cb,29:ud,30:wt,31:uo,32:dv,33:pt,34:tr,35:gw,36:cb,37:ud,38:wt,39:uo,40:dv,41:kj,42:us,43:ax,44:io,45:dd",
      "content": "//! C++ exception handling for the OCCT FFI boundary.\n//!\n//! When a C++ OCCT function throws an exception (typically a `Standard_Failure`\n//! subclass), the generated wrapper catches it and stores the exception info\n//! in a thread-local. The generated Rust wrappers detect exceptions either by\n//! checking for null return values (for pointer-returning functions) or by\n//! calling `check_exception()`, and then call `throw_pending_exception()` to\n//! convert the C++ exception into a Rust panic.\n\nuse std::ffi::{c_char, CStr};\n\nextern \"C\" {\n    fn occt_has_pending_exception() -> bool;\n    fn occt_pending_exception_message() -> *const c_char;\n    fn occt_pending_exception_type() -> *const c_char;\n    fn occt_clear_exception();\n}\n\n/// Panics with details of a pending OCCT C++ exception.\n///\n/// Called when an FFI wrapper detects an exception (e.g., null pointer from a\n/// function that returns a reference, or `check_exception` finds a pending\n/// exception). Reads exception details from thread-local storage and panics.\n///\n/// The panic message includes the FFI function name, C++ exception type, and\n/// exception message. The panic location points to the calling wrapper thanks\n/// to `#[track_caller]`. Set `RUST_BACKTRACE=1` for a full stack trace.\n#[cold]\n#[inline(never)]\n#[track_caller]\npub fn throw_pending_exception(fn_name: &str) -> ! {\n    let msg = unsafe {\n        let ptr = occt_pending_exception_message();\n        if ptr.is_null() {\n            \"<null>\"\n        } else {\n            CStr::from_ptr(ptr).to_str().unwrap_or(\"<invalid UTF-8>\")\n        }\n    };\n    let ty = unsafe {\n        let ptr = occt_pending_exception_type();\n        if ptr.is_null() {\n            \"<null>\"\n        } else {\n            CStr::from_ptr(ptr).to_str().unwrap_or(\"<invalid UTF-8>\")\n        }\n    };\n    unsafe { occt_clear_exception() };\n    panic!(\"OCCT C++ exception in {fn_name} ({ty}): {msg}\");\n}\n\n/// Check for pending OCCT C++ exceptions and panic if one is found.\n///\n/// Called after FFI calls for functions that don't return a pointer (void,\n/// primitive, enum returns). For pointer-returning functions, the wrappers\n/// check for null and call `throw_pending_exception` directly instead.\n#[inline(always)]\n#[track_caller]\npub fn check_exception(fn_name: &str) {\n    if unsafe { occt_has_pending_exception() } {\n        throw_pending_exception(fn_name);\n    }\n}\n"
    }
  ]
}

crate::check_exception(\\\"{}\\\"); }}\", raw_call, ffi_fn_name);\n    }\n\n    // All non-void paths: call the FFI, then detect exceptions.\n    // For paths returning a pointer (ref, OwnedPtr), we check for null locally\n    // to avoid an extra FFI call in the common (no-exception) case.\n    // For non-pointer returns (primitives, enums), we call check_exception().\n\n    if is_mut_ref_enum_return {\n        // &mut enum return: FFI returns *mut i32, cast to *mut EnumType and dereference\n        if let Some(enum_type) = is_enum {\n            return format!(\"{{ let __result = unsafe {{ {} }}; <details>
<summary>Reading all lines of <a href="../crates/opencascade-sys/tests/exception_handling.rs">exception_handling.rs</a></summary>

```
//! Tests for C++ exception handling across the FFI boundary.
//!
//! These tests verify that OCCT C++ exceptions are caught by the generated
//! wrappers and converted into Rust panics with meaningful messages.

/// Calling `TopoDS::Face()` on a non-Face shape throws `Standard_TypeMismatch`
/// in C++. Verify that this is caught and turned into a Rust panic.
#[test]
#[should_panic(expected = "Standard_TypeMismatch")]
fn face_shape_on_compound_panics_with_type_mismatch() {
    // Create a box — its .shape() returns a Compound (not a Face)
    let mut make_box = opencascade_sys::b_rep_prim_api::MakeBox::new_real3(10.0, 20.0, 30.0);
    let compound_shape = make_box.shape();

    // This should panic because compound_shape is a Compound, not a Face
    let _face = opencascade_sys::topo_ds::face_shape(compound_shape);
}
```
</details>

The test uses `expected = "Standard_TypeMismatch"` which should still be in the new message format `"OCCT C++ exception in TopoDS_face_shape (Standard_TypeMismatch): TopoDS::Face"`. ```
//! C++ exception handling for the OCCT FFI boundary.
//!
//! When a C++ OCCT function throws an exception (typically a `Standard_Failure`
//! subclass), the generated wrapper catches it and stores the exception info
//! in a thread-local. The generated Rust wrappers detect exceptions either by
//! checking for null return values (for pointer-returning functions) or by
//! calling `check_exception()`, and then call `throw_pending_exception()` to
//! convert the C++ exception into a Rust panic.

use std::ffi::{c_char, CStr};

extern "C" {
    fn occt_has_pending_exception() -> bool;
    fn occt_pending_exception_message() -> *const c_char;
    fn occt_pending_exception_type() -> *const c_char;
    fn occt_clear_exception();
}

/// Panics with details of a pending OCCT C++ exception.
///
/// Called when an FFI wrapper detects an exception (e.g., null pointer from a
/// function that returns a reference, or `check_exception` finds a pending
/// exception). Reads exception details from thread-local storage and panics.
///
/// The panic message includes the FFI function name, C++ exception type, and
/// exception message. The panic location points to the calling wrapper thanks
/// to `#[track_caller]`. Set `RUST_BACKTRACE=1` for a full stack trace.
#[cold]
#[inline(never)]
#[track_caller]
pub fn throw_pending_exception(fn_name: &str) -> ! {
    let msg = unsafe {
        let ptr = occt_pending_exception_message();
        if ptr.is_null() {
            "<null>"
        } else {
            CStr::from_ptr(ptr).to_str().unwrap_or("<invalid UTF-8>")
        }
    };
    let ty = unsafe {
        let ptr = occt_pending_exception_type();
        if ptr.is_null() {
            "<null>"
        } else {
            CStr::from_ptr(ptr).to_str().unwrap_or("<invalid UTF-8>")
        }
    };
    unsafe { occt_clear_exception() };
    panic!("OCCT C++ exception in {fn_name} ({ty}): {msg}");
}

/// Check for pending OCCT C++ exceptions and panic if one is found.
///
/// Called after FFI calls for functions that don't return a pointer (void,
/// primitive, enum returns). For pointer-returning functions, the wrappers
/// check for null and call `throw_pending_exception` directly instead.
#[inline(always)]
#[track_caller]
pub fn check_exception(fn_name: &str) {
    if unsafe { occt_has_pending_exception() } {
        throw_pending_exception(fn_name);
    }
}

```
</details>

```
pub(super) fn build_reexport_body(raw_call: &str, rt: Option<&ReturnTypeBinding>, ffi_fn_name: &str) -> String {
    let reexport_type = rt.map(|r| r.rust_reexport_type.as_str());
    let is_enum = rt.and_then(|r| r.enum_rust_type.as_deref());
    let needs_owned_ptr = rt.map_or(false, |r| r.needs_unique_ptr);
    let is_class_ptr_return = rt.map_or(false, |r| r.is_class_ptr_return);
    let is_mut_ref_enum_return = rt.map_or(false, |r| r.is_mut_ref_enum_return);

    // Void return: call, check, done
    if rt.is_none() || reexport_type.is_none() {
        return format!("{{ unsafe {{ {} }}; crate::check_exception(\"{}\"); }}", raw_call, ffi_fn_name);
    }

    // All non-void paths: call the FFI, then detect exceptions.
    // For paths returning a pointer (ref, OwnedPtr), we check for null locally
    // to avoid an extra FFI call in the common (no-exception) case.
    // For non-pointer returns (primitives, enums), we call check_exception().

    if is_mut_ref_enum_return {
        // &mut enum return: FFI returns *mut i32, cast to *mut EnumType and dereference
        if let Some(enum_type) = is_enum {
            return format!("{{ let __result = unsafe {{ {} }}; if __result.is_null() {{ crate::throw_pending_exception(\"{}\"); }} unsafe {{ &mut *(__result as *mut {}) }} }}", raw_call, ffi_fn_name, enum_type);
        } else {
            return format!("{{ let __result = unsafe {{ {} }}; if __result.is_null() {{ crate::throw_pending_exception(\"{}\"); }} unsafe {{ &mut *(__result) }} }}", raw_call, ffi_fn_name);
        }
    }
    if is_class_ptr_return {
        // Class pointer returns are bound as Option<&T> / Option<&mut T>.
        // The FFI returns a raw pointer; we null-check and convert.
        // For these, null is a valid "None" result, so we use check_exception.
        if let Some(rty) = reexport_type {
            if rty.starts_with("Option<&mut ") {
                return format!("{{ let __result = unsafe {{ {} }}; crate::check_exception(\"{}\"); if __result.is_null() {{ None }} else {{ Some(unsafe {{ &mut *__result }}) }} }}", raw_call, ffi_fn_name);
            }
        }
        return format!("{{ let __result = unsafe {{ {} }}; crate::check_exception(\"{}\"); if __result.is_null() {{ None }} else {{ Some(unsafe {{ &*__result }}) }} }}", raw_call, ffi_fn_name);
    }
    if let Some(enum_type) = is_enum {
        return format!("{{ let __result = unsafe {{ {} }}; crate::check_exception(\"{}\"); {}::try_from(__result).unwrap() }}", raw_call, ffi_fn_name, enum_type);
    }
    if needs_owned_ptr {
        return format!("{{ let __result = unsafe {{ {} }}; if __result.is_null() {{ crate::throw_pending_exception(\"{}\"); }} unsafe {{ crate::OwnedPtr::from_raw(__result) }} }}", raw_call, ffi_fn_name);
    }
    if let Some(rty) = reexport_type {
        if rty == "std::string::String" {
            // CString return: null check (was a const char* return from C++)
            return format!("{{ let __result = unsafe {{ {} }}; if __result.is_null() {{ crate::throw_pending_exception(\"{}\"); }} unsafe {{ std::ffi::CStr::from_ptr(__result) }}.to_string_lossy().into_owned() }}", raw_call, ffi_fn_name);
        } else if rty.starts_with("&mut ") {
            // Mutable reference return: null = exception
            return format!("{{ let __result = unsafe {{ {} }}; if __result.is_null() {{ crate::throw_pending_exception(\"{}\"); }} unsafe {{ &mut *(__result) }} }}", raw_call, ffi_fn_name);
        } else if rty.starts_with('&') {
            // Immutable reference return: null = exception
            return format!("{{ let __result = unsafe {{ {} }}; if __result.is_null() {{ crate::throw_pending_exception(\"{}\"); }} unsafe {{ &*(__result) }} }}", raw_call, ffi_fn_name);
        }
    }
    // Simple passthrough (primitives, bool, etc.) — must use check_exception
    format!("{{ let __result = unsafe {{ {} }}; crate::check_exception(\"{}\"); __result }}", raw_call, ffi_fn_name)
}
pub fn emit_reexport_class(bi
... (truncated, 4764 chars)

```
//! Tests for C++ exception handling across the FFI boundary.
//!
//! These tests verify that OCCT C++ exceptions are caught by the generated
//! wrappers and converted into Rust panics with meaningful messages.

/// Calling `TopoDS::Face()` on a non-Face shape throws `Standard_TypeMismatch`
/// in C++. Verify that this is caught and turned into a Rust panic.
#[test]
#[should_panic(expected = "OCCT C++ exception in TopoDS_face_shape (Standard_TypeMismatch)")]
fn face_shape_on_compound_panics_with_type_mismatch() {
    // Create a box — its .shape() returns a Compound (not a Face)
    let mut make_box = opencascade_sys::b_rep_prim_api::MakeBox::new_real3(10.0, 20.0, 30.0);
    let compound_shape = make_box.shape();

    // This should panic because compound_shape is a Compound, not a Face
    let _face = opencascade_sys::topo_ds::face_shape(compound_shape);
}
```
</details>

All builds and tests pass. Here's the before/after comparison:

## Before/After Comparison

| Metric | Before | After | Delta |
|--------|--------|-------|-------|
| wrappers.cpp | 36,879,908 B | 36,879,908 B | 0 (unchanged — C++ side unchanged) |
| All .rs files | 117,020,218 B | 127,752,564 B | **+10,732,346 B (+9.2%)** |
| generated/ dir | 147 MB | 158 MB | **+11 MB (+7.5%)** |
| Compile time (clean) | 61.6s | 68.9s | **+7.3s (+11.9%)** |
| .rlib | 642,926,320 B | 707,039,552 B | **+64,113,232 B (+10.0%)** |
| .rmeta | 155,124,805 B | 155,431,103 B | **+306,298 B (+0.2%)** |
| C++ .a | 150,973,960 B | 150,973,960 B | 0 (unchanged) |

### Analysis

The .rs files grew ~9.2% and the .rlib grew 10% due to:
- **Function name string literals** — every wrapper now passes a `"fn_name"` string to `check_exception()` or `throw_pending_exception()`
- **Null-check code** — `if __result.is_null() { crate::throw_pending_exception("..."); }` is longer than the previous bare `crate::check_exception();`

### What we gained
1. **Better panic messages** — panics now include the FFI function name (e.g., `"OCCT C++ exception in TopoDS_face_shape (Standard_TypeMismatch): ..."`)
2. **Better panic locations** — `#[track_caller]` propagation means the panic location points to the generated wrapper file/line, not exception.rs
3. **More efficient common case for pointer returns** — null check on a local variable instead of an FFI call to `occt_has_pending_exception()`. This covers reference returns, constructors, OwnedPtr returns, CString returns.
4. **Cold path not inlined** — `throw_pending_exception` is `#[cold] #[inline(never)]`, keeping the exception-handling code out of the hot path. `check_exception` is `#[inline(always)]` so only the lightweight bool check gets inlined.

The .rmeta (type metadata) barely changed (+0.2%), and the C++ .a is identical since we didn't modify the C++ emission. The compile time increase is mostly from the Rust compiler processing the 10% more source.

*2026-02-25 14:59*

### Assistant — Claude Sonnet 4.6

> 💭 The user is asking about a hypothetical alternative design where instead of passing function name strings like `"TopoDS_face_shape"` to `check_exception`/`throw_pending_exception`, we would embed `panic!()` calls directly in the wrappers with a message retrieved from C++.
> 
> Let me think through this carefully:
> 
> **Current approach:**
> ```rust
> if __result.is_null() { crate::throw_pending_exception("TopoDS_face_shape"); }
> ```
> - Each wrapper has a string literal like `"TopoDS_face_shape"` embedded
> - The string literal is stored in the data segment (read-only)
> 
> 
> - These strings aren't huge, but there are ~100K+ of them
> 
> **Proposed approach:**
> ```rust
> if __result.threw_exception {
>     panic!(crate::pending_exception_message(__result));
> }
> ```
> 
> Now I'm considering what happens with `panic!()` at compile time. A static string literal in `panic!()` compiles very efficiently with the location hardcoded, but using a runtime string value like `panic!("{}", runtime_value)` generates much more code because it needs to format the message dynamically.
> 
> Comparing the two approaches...
> 
> with embedded string constants in each wrapper function, those literals live in the `.rodata` section and don't bloat the code itself—just a pointer and length get passed to the exception handler. But `#[track_caller]` with function names would embed location information differently, so I need to figure out which actually saves more binary space overall. is much smaller than a full string literal, so the tradeoff depends on how many wrapper functions exist and whether the file path itself is already deduplicated across all panic sites. The key insight is that using inline `panic!()` without embedding function name strings would save space in both source and binary—we'd eliminate those string literals from `.rodata`. The `#[track_caller]` location data (file and line number) would still be captured automatically, giving us stack information without needing the function name baked into each wrapper. So the trade-off is: we lose the explicit function name in the panic message but reclaim the space those string constants were consuming, though we'd still have the overhead of the `panic!()` macro itself generating code at each call site. The real tradeoff is between the binary size cost of embedding function name strings across hundreds of thousands of wrapper functions versus the code bloat from inlining panic machinery at each call site. Since `throw_pending_exception` is already marked cold and never-inlined, the current approach minimizes panic overhead—the only waste is those function name literals in .rodata, which could add up to a few megabytes. Removing them would save some space, but the proposed redesign with a `__result.threw_exception` field represents a much larger structural change than just eliminating the strings.

The size would likely **go up slightly** or stay the same, not go down. Here's why:

**The current approach:**
```rust
if __result.is_null() { crate::throw_pending_exception("TopoDS_face_shape"); }
```
In `.text`, this is just one `call` instruction (the string literal is a pointer constant). All the panic infrastructure — , `PanicInfo` setup, unwinding — lives inside the single `#[cold] #[inline(never)]` `throw_pending_exception`, shared across all call sites.

**The proposed approach:**
```rust
if __result.threw_exception {
    panic!(crate::pending_exception_message(__result));
}
```
`panic!(runtime_str)` expands to `panic!("{}", runtime_str)` which goes through `format_args!()` — that's format string setup and potential allocation inlined at **every single call site**, not pulled into one shared function.

**What actually drives the current size increase:**

The ~10% growth comes from the function name string literals (e.g., `"TopoDS_face_shape"`) being written into the generated `.rs` source and then stored in `.rodata`. Removing them *would* save that space. But you'd lose the function name from the panic message — you'd only get the wrapper's file:line from `#[track_caller]`.

**The better tradeoff if size matters:** Keep the current structure but drop the `fn_name` argument and rely solely on the `#[track_caller]` location. That saves the string constants while preserving `#[cold] #[inline(never)]` on the cold path:

```rust
if __result.is_null() { crate::throw_pending_exception(); }
// panic would say: "OCCT C++ exception (Standard_TypeMismatch): TopoDS::Face"
// at generated/topo_ds.rs:102  ← you still know the function from the location
```

//! C++ wrapper code generation
//!
//! Generates extern "C" wrapper functions for all OCCT methods:
//! - Constructors (using placement new or heap allocation)
//! - Return-by-value methods
//! - Static methods
//! - Overloaded methods
//!
//! All wrapper functions use extern "C" linkage for direct FFI access.

use crate::model::{ParsedClass, Type};
use crate::resolver::SymbolTable;
use std::collections::HashSet;
use std::fmt::Write;

fn collect_handle_types(classes: &[&ParsedClass], handle_able_classes: &HashSet<String>) -> Vec<(String, String)> {
    let mut handles = HashSet::new();

    for class in classes {
        // Add Handle type for classes that are transient (can be wrapped in Handle)
        // Handle types with protected destructors are included because Handle<T>
        // manages lifetime via reference counting, not direct delete.
        if handle_able_classes.contains(&class.name) {
            handles.insert(class.name.clone());
        }

        for method in &class.methods {
            collect_type_handles(&method.return_type, &mut handles);
            for param in &method.params {
                collect_type_handles(&Some(param.ty.clone()), &mut handles);
            }
        }

        for method in &class.static_methods {
            collect_type_handles(&method.return_type, &mut handles);
            for param in &method.params {
                collect_type_handles(&Some(param.ty.clone()), &mut handles);
            }
        }

        for ctor in &class.constructors {
            for param in &ctor.params {
                collect_type_handles(&Some(param.ty.clone()), &mut handles);
            }
        }
    }

    let mut result: Vec<_> = handles
        .into_iter()
        .filter(|inner_class| {

// ========================
// Exception handling
// ========================

#include <cxxabi.h>

namespace {
struct OcctExceptionInfo {
    bool pending = false;
    char message[2048] = {};
    char type_name[256] = {};
};
thread_local OcctExceptionInfo occt_last_exception;
}

static void occt_store_exception(const char* msg, const char* type_name) {
    auto& ex = occt_last_exception;
    ex.pending = true;
    if (msg) {
        std::strncpy(ex.message, msg, sizeof(ex.message) - 1);
        ex.message[sizeof(ex.message) - 1] = '\0';
    } else {
        ex.message[0] = '\0';
    }
    // Demangle C++ type name for readable panic messages
    if (type_name) {
        int status = 0;
        char* demangled = abi::__cxa_demangle(type_name, nullptr, nullptr, &status);
        if (status == 0 && demangled) {
            std::strncpy(ex.type_name, demangled, sizeof(ex.type_name) - 1);
            ex.type_name[sizeof(ex.type_name) - 1] = '\0';
            std::free(demangled);
        } else {
            std::strncpy(ex.type_name, type_name, sizeof(ex.type_name) - 1);
            ex.type_name[sizeof(ex.type_name) - 1] = '\0';
            std::free(demangled);
        }
    } else {
        ex.type_name[0] = '\0';
    }
}

extern "C" bool occt_has_pending_exception() {
    return occt_last_exception.pending;
}

extern "C" const char* occt_pending_exception_message() {
    return occt_last_exception.message;
}

extern "C" const char* occt_pending_exception_type() {
    return occt_last_exception.type_name;
}

extern "C" void occt_clear_exception() {
    occt_last_exception.pending = false;
}

#define OCCT_CATCH_AND_STORE \
    catch (const Standard_Failure& e) { \
        occt_store_exception(e.GetMessageString(), typeid(e).name()); \
    } catch (const std::exception& e) { \
        occt_store_exception(e.what(), typeid(e).name()); \
    } catch (...) { \
        occt_store_exception("unknown C++ exception", "unknown"); \
    }

"#);

<details>

```
/// Generate wrappers for all namespace-level free functions from pre-computed FunctionBindings
fn generate_function_wrappers(
    output: &mut String,
    function_bindings: &[super::bindings::FunctionBinding],
) {
    if function_bindings.is_empty() {
        return;
    }

    // Group functions by namespace
    let mut by_namespace: std::collections::HashMap<&str, Vec<&super::bindings::FunctionBinding>> =
        std::collections::HashMap::new();
    for func in function_bindings {
        by_namespace
            .entry(&func.namespace)
            .or_default()
            .push(func);
    }

    let mut namespaces: Vec<&&str> = by_namespace.keys().collect();
    namespaces.sort();

    for namespace in namespaces {
        let namespace_functions = &by_namespace[namespace];

        writeln!(output, "// ========================").unwrap();
        writeln!(output, "// {} namespace functions", namespace).unwrap();
        writeln!(output, "// ========================").unwrap();

        for func in namespace_functions {
            let wrapper_name = &func.cpp_wrapper_name;

            // Build param declarations from pre-computed cpp_type
            let params_cpp: Vec<String> = func.params.iter()
                .map(|p| format!("{} {}", p.cpp_type, p.cpp_name))
                .collect();
            let params_str = params_cpp.join(", ");

            // Build argument expressions from pre-computed cpp_arg_expr
            let args: Vec<String> = func.params.iter()
                .map(|p| p.cpp_arg_expr.clone())
                .collect();
            let args_str = args.join(", ");

            let call = format!("{}::{}({})", namespace, func.short_name, args_str);

            // Determine return pattern from pre-computed return type binding
            if let Some(ref rt) = func.return_type {
                let ret_type_cpp = rt.ffi_cpp_return_type();
                let return_stmt = rt.format_cpp_return_stmt(&call, false);
                writeln!(
                    output,
                    "extern \"C\" {} {}({}) {{",
                    ret_type_cpp, wrapper_name, params_str
                ).unwrap();
                writeln!(output, "    try {{ {} }}", return_stmt).unwrap();
                writeln!(output, "    OCCT_CATCH_AND_STORE").unwrap();
                if let Some(default) = super::bindings::cpp_error_return_value(&ret_type_cpp) {
                    writeln!(output, "    return {};", default).unwrap();
                }
                writeln!(output, "}}").unwrap();
            } else {
                writeln!(
                    output,
                    "extern \"C\" void {}({}) {{",
                    wrapper_name, params_str
                ).unwrap();
                writeln!(output, "    try {{ {}; }}", call).unwrap();
                writeln!(output, "    OCCT_CATCH_AND_STORE").unwrap();
                writeln!(output, "}}").unwrap();
            }
        }
        writeln!(output).unwrap();
    }
}

fn collect_function_required_headers(
    function_bindings: &[super::bindings::FunctionBinding],
    known_headers: &HashSet<String>,
) -> Vec<String> {
    let mut headers = HashSet::new();

    for func in function_bindings {
        let ns_header = format!("{}.hxx", func.namespace);
        if known_headers.is_empty() || known_headers.contains(&ns_header) {
            headers.insert(ns_header);
        }

        for header in &func.cpp_headers {
            headers.insert(header.clone());
        }
    }

    let mut result: Vec<_> = headers.into_iter().collect();
    result.sort();
    result
}

fn extend_unique_headers(headers: &mut Vec<String>, additional_headers: impl IntoIterator<Item = String>) {
    for header in additional_headers {
        if !headers.contains(&header) {
            headers.push(header);
        }
    }
}

pub fn generate_wrappers(
    all_classes: &[&ParsedClass],
    collections: &[super::collections::CollectionInfo],
    known_headers: &HashSe
... (truncated, 4043 chars)

> 💭 This is a very large refactor. There are many call sites in bindings.rs that generate C++ wrapper code with try/catch patterns. Let me think about the most efficient approach.
> 
> There are roughly two categories of C++ wrapper output:
> 1. **`cpp.rs`**: Free function wrappers (lines ~190-210) — about 2 call sites
> 2. **`bindings.rs`**: Class method wrappers — ~30+ call sites
> 
> And on the Rust side:
> 1. **`bindings.rs`**: `build_reexport_body()` — handles all the Rust wrapper patterns
> 
> 
> 2. **`bindings.rs`**: Hardcoded format strings for constructors, upcasts, etc.
> 3. **`rust.rs`**: Free function wrappers
> 
> This is going to be a massive change. I need to design a unified exception handling system that works across all these call sites.
> 
> The core idea is to use an `OcctExcInfo` struct that gets returned from every C++ wrapper — null pointer means success, non-null means there's an exception message allocated on the Rust side. I'll define a helper macro in C++ that handles the try/catch boilerplate and formats exception messages with demangled type names. On the Rust side, I'll have a callback that allocates memory for exception strings and a panic function that cleans up and reports the error.
> 
> The wrapper patterns differ based on return type: void functions return just the exception info, pointer-returning functions use an output parameter, and primitive-returning functions do the same. I need to be careful about zero-sized allocations though — I should handle that edge case properly.
> 
> Now I'm mapping out the concrete implementation steps. I need to rewrite `exception.rs` entirely with the new struct and functions, update `cpp.rs` to remove the old thread-local exception storage and add the new macro and helper, then systematically go through `bindings.rs` to change how all the wrapper signatures and bodies are generated. The FFI declarations will need new signatures too, and `rust.rs` will need updates for the new wrapper pattern.
> 
> Looking at the current code flow, there are many different wrapper emission patterns scattered throughout. Rather than modifying each one individually, I should identify the systematic approach — every wrapper needs to follow the same exception-handling pattern regardless of what it's wrapping.
> 
> The key realization is that if I change the C++ return type to always be `OcctExcInfo` and update how return statements are formatted to write to an out parameter, then most of the C++ emission sites will work with minimal changes. The pattern shifts from returning the actual type directly to always returning exception info, with the actual result written to an out parameter for non-void functions.
> 
> Now I need to trace through how the FFI declarations get generated—specifically how `ReturnTypeBinding` feeds into the Rust FFI signatures. The `ffi_cpp_return_type()` method will now always return `OcctExcInfo`, and I'll need to adjust how the Rust FFI side constructs its function signatures to include the out parameter when needed.

pub struct ReturnTypeBinding {
    /// Type as it appears in ffi.rs
    pub rust_ffi_type: String,
    /// Type as it appears in re-export impl
    pub rust_reexport_type: String,
    /// C++ type for wrappers.hxx
    pub cpp_type: String,
    /// Whether the C++ return needs std::unique_ptr wrapping
    pub needs_unique_ptr: bool,
    /// If this is an enum return, the original C++ enum name (for static_cast)
    pub enum_cpp_name: Option<String>,
    /// If this is a value enum return, the qualified Rust enum type
    pub enum_rust_type: Option<String>,
    /// If this is a raw pointer return to a known class type (const T* / T*)
    pub is_class_ptr_return: bool,
    /// If this is a mutable reference to an enum (EnumType& → *mut i32).
    /// The C++ wrapper returns int32_t* via reinterpret_cast.
    pub is_mut_ref_enum_return: bool,
}

impl ReturnTypeBinding {
    /// Compute the C++ return type string for the extern "C" wrapper signature.
    /// Handles unique_ptr (pointer), mut ref enum (int32_t*), value enum (int32_t),
    /// and passthrough cases.
    pub fn ffi_cpp_return_type(&self) -> String {
        if self.needs_unique_ptr {
            format!("{}*", self.cpp_type)
        } else if self.is_mut_ref_enum_return {
            "int32_t*".to_string()
        } else if self.enum_cpp_name.is_some() {
            "int32_t".to_string()
        } else if self.cpp_type.contains('&') {
            // Convert reference returns to pointer returns for exception safety.
            // At the ABI level, C++ references and pointers are identical.
            self.cpp_type.replace('&', "*")
        } else {
            self.cpp_type.clone()
        }
    }

    /// Generate the C++ return statement for a call expression.
    /// Returns the statement text (without leading indent or trailing newline).
    ///
    /// When `assign_to_variable` is true, generates `auto result_ = <expr>;`
    /// instead of `return <expr>;` (used in multi-statement wrappers).
    pub fn format_cpp_return_stmt(&self, call_expr: &str, assign_to_variable: bool) -> String {
        let is_ref_return = !self.needs_unique_ptr
            && !self.is_mut_ref_enum_return
            && self.enum_cpp_name.is_none()
            && self.cpp_type.contains('&');

        let expr = if self.is_mut_ref_enum_return {
            format!("reinterpret_cast<int32_t*>(&({}))", call_expr)
        } else if self.needs_unique_ptr {
            format!("new {}({})", self.cpp_type, call_expr)
        } else if self.enum_cpp_name.is_some() {
            format!("static_cast<int32_t>({})", call_expr)
        } else if is_ref_return {
            // Reference return converted to pointer: take address of result
            format!("&({})", call_expr)
        } else {
            call_expr.to_string()
        };

        if assign_to_variable {
            format!("auto result_ = {};", expr)
        } else {
            format!("return {};", expr)
        }
    }
}

pub struct ResolvedParamBinding {
    pub name: String,
    /// Rust parameter name (keyword-escaped)
    pub rust_name: String,
    pub rust_ffi_type: String,
    /// Type as it appears in re-export impl (e.g. "&crate::ffi::gp_Pnt" or enum type)
    pub rust_reexport_type: String,
    pub cpp_type: String,
    pub cpp_arg_expr: String,
    /// If this is a value enum param, the qualified Rust enum type
    pub enum_rust_type: Option<String>,
    /// If this is a &mut enum output param, the C++ enum name (for preamble/postamble)
    pub mut_ref_enum_cpp_name: Option<String>,
    /// If this is a &mut enum output param, the qualified Rust enum type
    pub mut_ref_enum_rust_type: Option<String>,
    /// If this is a nullable pointer param (T* = NULL or const T* = NULL)
    pub is_nullable_ptr: bool,
    /// If this is a non-nullable class pointer param (const T* / T* where T is a known class)
    pub is_class_ptr: bool,
}

```
pub(super) fn cpp_error_return_value(cpp_return_type: &str) -> Option<&'static str> {
    if cpp_return_type == "void" {
        None
    } else if cpp_return_type.contains('*') {
        Some("nullptr")
    } else if cpp_return_type == "bool" {
        Some("false")
    } else {
        // Covers int32_t, double, float, size_t, Standard_Real, etc.
        Some("0")
    }
}

/// Emit C++ wrapper code for a single class from pre-computed ClassBindings.
///
/// Produces C++ wrapper code for a class
/// and its 10+ sub-functions, but consumes the pre-computed IR instead
/// of re-deriving decisions.
pub fn emit_cpp_class(bindings: &ClassBindings) -> String {
    use std::fmt::Write;

    let mut output = String::new();
    let ffi_cn = &bindings.cpp_name;  // Rust-safe flattened name (for FFI function names)
    let cn = &bindings.cpp_qualified_name;  // C++ qualified name (for C++ type expressions)

    // POD structs don't need C++ wrappers, but we generate a sizeof helper
    // so Rust tests can verify layout compatibility at runtime,
    // and a destructor so CppDeletable can be implemented (needed when returned by pointer).
    if bindings.is_pod_struct {
        writeln!(output, "// sizeof helper for POD struct {}", cn).unwrap();
        writeln!(output, "extern \"C\" size_t {}_sizeof() {{ return sizeof({}); }}", ffi_cn, cn).unwrap();
        writeln!(output, "extern \"C\" void {}_destructor({}* self_) {{ delete self_; }}", ffi_cn, cn).unwrap();
        writeln!(output).unwrap();
        return output;
    }

    writeln!(output, "// ========================").unwrap();
    writeln!(output, "// {} wrappers", cn).unwrap();
    writeln!(output, "// ========================").unwrap();
    writeln!(output).unwrap();

    // 1. Constructor wrappers (skip convenience — they are Rust-only)
    for ctor in bindings.constructors.iter().filter(|c| c.convenience_of.is_none()) {
        let params_cpp: Vec<String> = ctor
            .params
            .iter()
            .map(|p| format!("{} {}", p.cpp_type, p.cpp_name))
            .collect();
        let params_str = params_cpp.join(", ");
